lesson_contract.objective: Build capability in your current role through deliberate practice, useful feedback, coachability, and reliable execution.
objective_points: Use a practice and feedback loop
Connect capability to current role expectations
Confirm any increase in ownership with your manager and current written role plan
lesson_contract.transition_spoken_text: Take the Career Growth Path checkpoint next. Then complete the Mission Control and Growth Capstone.

required_teaching_beats.description: Development starts by getting reliable at the work assigned now.
Accurate records and complete handoffs make capability visible.
More complex situations require deliberate practice and review.
Specific feedback is practiced, applied, and reviewed in real work.
The learner listens before defending the first approach.
Helping peers and sharing useful context strengthens the work.
Development stays connected to the quality and usefulness of business outcomes.
New ownership requires manager confirmation and the current written role plan.
The role sheet, offer letter, and manager direction control current expectations.
The close directs the learner to the checkpoint and capstone.

required_examples.description: A thin handoff is revised into a complete record.
A sensitive multi-owner situation becomes a practice target.
The learner applies feedback after rushing a discovery question.
The learner helps a peer without claiming authority over their work.
A manager confirms new ownership in the current written role plan.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: hitting your numbers for 90-plus days, you move up
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Use current written role expectations and never promise advancement on a fixed timeline.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: The earning potential is significantly higher because you're earning commission
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Remove compensation comparisons and point learners to current written sources.

forbidden_language_removals.exact_source_language: Some people advance in six months. Some take a year.
forbidden_language_removals.replacement_rule: Remove example promotion timelines from reusable training.
Do not promise a role ladder or named advancement path.

scenes.title: Build capability where you are
scenes.spoken_text: Development starts with the work assigned to you now. Learn the seller conversations, the current process, the records, and the handoffs that your role sheet expects. Reliability is visible in the ordinary work. The notes are accurate. The next action is clear. A commitment gets completed. Start there. Ask which part of the work would make the biggest difference if it became more consistent, then practice that part deliberately. A new title or a training example cannot substitute for becoming dependable at the responsibilities already in front of you.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea in a direct medium shot beside a current-work folder
visual_plan.editor_note: Open on capability without a ladder or title.

scenes.title: Make the quality of your work visible
scenes.spoken_text: Clean output gives you something real to improve. Imagine a handoff with a vague motivation note, no decision makers, and no next action. Go back to the conversation, identify the missing facts, and rebuild the record so another person can use it. A trustworthy page gives the next person enough detail to understand the seller's situation and take the right action. Extra activity on the page does not help. Reliable records and useful handoffs show what you can do without a promise about what comes next.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea comparing a thin record with a complete handoff
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no score, pay, or advancement graphic.

scenes.title: Practice the situations that stretch you
scenes.spoken_text: Growth often shows up as better judgment in harder situations. A property may have several owners who disagree, a seller may be grieving, or a financial deadline may make the conversation sensitive. Choose a skill inside that situation. It could be slowing down, identifying every decision maker, or knowing when to pause for qualified review. Practice the skill, use it in real work when appropriate, and review the result with your manager.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea reviewing a sensitive multi-owner scenario
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the people neutral and avoid a named role or promised outcome.

scenes.title: Turn feedback into another attempt
scenes.spoken_text: Ask for feedback that points to a behavior you can change. Suppose you rushed past a discovery answer and moved to the next script line. Your manager may ask you to restate what you heard and leave more room before the follow-up question. Practice that moment out loud. Use the correction in the next appropriate conversation, then bring back an example. Feedback becomes useful when it changes what you do, not when it simply sounds agreeable.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a listen, practice, apply, review loop
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no fixed repetition count or timeline.

scenes.title: Listen before defending the first approach
scenes.spoken_text: Coachability can feel uncomfortable because useful feedback often arrives where you thought you were already doing well. Listen through it. Ask for the exact moment or record that shows the gap. If you disagree, explain what you saw after you understand the concern. Then test the correction in the work. Defensiveness protects the first version. Curiosity gives you a chance to build a better version and show what changed.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea seated in a calm coaching conversation
visual_plan.editor_note: Keep the posture open and realistic.

scenes.title: Contribute without taking over
scenes.spoken_text: Development also shows in how you help the people around you. Share a question that worked, point a peer to the current SOP, or help them find a missing record. Do not rewrite someone else's responsibilities or present your habit as the rule. For example, if a peer is stuck on a handoff, help them compare it with the current checklist and let the assigned owner make the decision. Useful support strengthens the process without creating unofficial authority.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea and a peer reviewing a generic checklist together
visual_plan.editor_note: Show equal status and no job-title labels.

scenes.title: Confirm changes in writing
scenes.spoken_text: Getting better does not automatically change your responsibilities. If the business needs you to own different work, your manager should confirm it and the change should appear in the current written role plan. Review the role sheet and offer letter issued to you. Ask how the new ownership will be measured and where questions should go. Another person's duties, an old course example, or an informal compliment cannot redefine what you are responsible for. Development stays concrete when you can name the skill, the feedback, and the change in your work.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea beside a newly confirmed written ownership card
visual_plan.editor_note: Use no title, pay term, timeline, or promotion arrow.

scenes.title: Continue into the capstone
scenes.spoken_text: Take the Career Growth Path checkpoint next. Then complete the Mission Control and Growth Capstone.

visual_plan.shot: Andrea centered in a medium shot

edit_operations.source_problem: The source combines useful capability and coachability teaching with a role ladder, fixed timelines, compensation comparisons, and advancement promises. The full replacement retains the substantive development lesson while binding ownership to current written sources.
